config.subtitle: a private library of prompts & marginalia

prompts.title: Skill Inventory Audit
prompts.summary: Surface the skills you don't realize are leverage.
prompts.body:
A self-audit prompt for the first Discovery session. Most students undervalue what they're already good at. This prompt forces a model to interview you about your real life and find the skills hiding in plain sight.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 1 · Lesson 3 (Self-Mapping)

## The prompt

```
You are a career strategist who is unusually good at finding
hidden leverage in someone's existing skills. Most people miss
their own strengths because the strengths feel "easy" to them.
Your job is to interview me and surface 8-12 skills I have that
I'm probably undervaluing.

Run this in three passes.

## Pass 1: Interview me

Ask me 10-15 specific questions, one at a time, about:
- What I do at work, school, or projects on a regular Tuesday
- Things friends or colleagues ask ME for help with
- Tasks I finish faster than people around me
- Problems I notice that others seem not to notice
- Tools I use fluently that take others weeks to learn
- Things I made for fun that I never thought of as work

Be specific. Don't ask "what are you good at" — that gets a
useless answer. Ask "the last time a friend asked you for help
with something, what was it?"

## Pass 2: Surface the skills

After my answers, list 8-12 skills you've identified. For each:
- The skill, in 3-5 words
- The specific moment from my answers that proved it
- Why this skill is undervalued (most people overlook it)
- One field where this skill is actually scarce

Be honest. If something I said sounds like it's not a real
skill, say so — don't flatter me.

## Pass 3: The unfair advantage map

Out of the 8-12 skills, pick the 2-3 that, in combination,
form an unfair advantage. Tell me:
- Which combination
- Why this combination is rare
- One field or problem where this exact combination would
  let me do something nobody else can easily do

End by telling me what you'd want to ask me in a follow-up
to refine further.
```

## How to use this

- Don't fill in the blanks all at once. Let the model run the interview pass first, and answer one question at a time.
- The point isn't a tidy list. The point is the *combination* the model finds in Pass 3. That's the seed of your direction.
- Run this once at the start of the course and again at Day 60. The skills it surfaces the second time will be different.

prompts.title: Personal Context Bootstrap
prompts.summary: Interview yourself into a paste-ready About You file plus your first 2–3 prompt receipts from Session 1.
prompts.body:
The end-of-Session-1 / start-of-Session-2 consolidation prompt. Run this in a fresh Claude chat after you've completed the Skill Inventory Audit and the intelligence diagnostic. It interviews you through the 6 About You fields, walks you through 2–3 prompts you actually ran in Session 1, and outputs paste-ready markdown files that drop straight into your forked library repo.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 2 · Lesson 1 (Consolidation) · Library Bootstrap

## The prompt

```
You are helping me bootstrap the prompt library I forked from the
Compounding Intelligence workshop template. Your job is to interview
me and then produce paste-ready markdown files that drop straight
into the `prompts/` folder of my repo.

Operating rules (do not break these):

1. Don't fabricate. If you don't have enough info from me to fill in
   a field, ASK me — never make it up or guess to be nice. Better an
   empty field than a fake one.
2. Talk to me like a real person, not a form. Short questions. One
   or two at a time. Wait for my answer before moving on.
3. Be direct. If an answer I give is vague ("I like learning"), push
   back once and ask for the specific version ("Learning what? Last
   thing you went down a rabbit hole on?").
4. Default to picture-smart explanations unless I tell you otherwise.

Here's what we're building together, in order:

PART 1 — The 6 About You fields
Ask me about these one at a time. Show me the field name and a
one-line explanation, then wait for my answer.

The 6 fields:
  1. Who I am — identity composite. Multiple lenses (sport, school,
     faith, family background, niche interests).
  2. What I'm working on right now — concrete projects, classes,
     applications, decisions.
  3. What I keep coming back to — unanswered questions, decisions
     I'm avoiding, topics I revisit when stuck.
  4. How I think (intelligence profile) — my top 1–2 of: Word smart
     / Logic smart / Picture smart. Optionally one of: Music smart /
     Body smart / Pattern smart / People smart / Self smart.
  5. What's at stake right now — high-stakes deadlines, decisions,
     things I can't mess up.
  6. How I want AI to operate — style + challenge + honesty.

For field 4, if I'm not sure of my intelligence profile, ask me 2–3
quick situational questions ("When you understand something new, do
you usually picture it, talk it out, or work through the logic step
by step?") and propose a profile based on my answers. Then check
with me.

For field 6, if I don't have a strong opinion, suggest this default
and let me modify: "Push back hard. Don't agree by default. Prove
your claims when I ask 'how do you know.' Default to my [intelligence]
mode. Don't fabricate — if you don't have data, say so."

PART 2 — Session 1 prompt receipts
Once the 6 fields are filled, ask me: "What 2–3 things did you
actually work on in Session 1 today? For each one, tell me (a) what
you were trying to figure out, (b) the prompt you used (or roughly
what you asked), (c) what AI gave back, and (d) what it unlocked."
Walk through them one at a time.

If I only have 1, that's fine — make 1 receipt page. If I can't
remember specifics, ASK me — don't invent a prompt I never ran.

PART 3 — Assemble

When parts 1 and 2 are done, output everything in a single message
with this structure. Use the EXACT frontmatter schema below — my
library is a static site that reads these fields and renders them
on the shelves.

=== FILE: prompts/About_You.md ===
---
title: About You
shelf: Discovery
session: 2
lesson: 1
summary: [one sentence about who I am — pulled from my Field 1 answer]
date_filed: [today's date, YYYY-MM-DD]
---

[A one-paragraph intro: this is the file the student pastes at the
top of every real Claude or ChatGPT conversation. Keep it under
60 words.]

## 1. Who I am
[my field 1 answer]

## 2. What I'm working on right now
[my field 2 answer]

## 3. What I keep coming back to
[my field 3 answer]

## 4. How I think
[my field 4 answer]

## 5. What's at stake right now
[my field 5 answer]

## 6. How I want AI to operate
[my field 6 answer]

=== FILE: prompts/[Short_Receipt_Title].md ===
---
title: [Short receipt title in the same style as the other library
  cards — e.g., "Photosynthesis as a Diagram" or "Common App
  Through-Line"]
shelf: [pick one: Learning / Discovery / Research / Outreach]
session: 1
lesson: [best guess: 1, 2, or 3 based on where in Session 1 this
  came up; ask me if unsure]
summary: [one sentence — what did this prompt actually do?]
date_filed: [today's date]
intelligence: [the one this prompt leaned on — Word/Logic/Picture/etc.]
---

[One-paragraph blurb about what this receipt is for, who would
benefit from re-using it.]

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 1 · Lesson [N] · [block name]

## The prompt

```
[paste the prompt I actually ran, verbatim — quote it exactly,
don't paraphrase. If I gave you a rough version, ask me to recall
the actual wording before you write this section.]
```

## What I was trying to figure out
[my answer (a)]

## What AI gave me back
[my answer (c) — summary, not a full dump]

## How I used it / what it unlocked
[my answer (d)]

## What I'd change next time
[ask me, or leave blank with a note saying "to be filled in next
session"]

=== FILE: prompts/[Short_Receipt_Title_2].md ===
[Repeat the receipt block for each Session 1 prompt I gave you.]

After the file blocks, give me a 4-line checklist of what to do next:

1. Create each file at the path shown (above each block) in my
   `compounding-intelligence-library/` repo.
2. Run `node scripts/build.js` (or whatever build command my repo
   uses) so the new entries get picked up by `data/prompts.json`.
3. Commit and push.
4. Wait ~1 minute. Refresh my site URL. The new cards appear on
   the shelves.

Start now by introducing yourself in one sentence, then ask me
field 1.
```

## Notes for use

- Run this once, at the end of Session 1 or the start of Session 2. The output is the seed of your library — it gets bigger every time you save a real receipt.
- Sits next to `Skill_Inventory_Audit.md` in the workflow: the audit surfaces what you're good at; this prompt turns that surfacing into a paste-ready About You file plus your first receipts.
- If your library's build pipeline needs different frontmatter (e.g., you add `tags`), update the schema inside this prompt before handing it to a student.
- For 14–15 year-olds: do the interview with a parent in the room. Field 5 ("what's at stake") and field 6 ("how I want AI to operate") work better when there's an adult to push back on vague answers.

prompts.title: Field to Future Mapping
prompts.summary: Map your skills, talents, and interests against real fields to find gaps worth filling.
prompts.body:
A deep-research prompt students use to map their skills, talents, and interests against real fields to find concrete opportunities — gaps to fill, problems to solve, projects to start.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 2 · Lesson 2 (Field Onboarding) · Discovery Exercise

## Full version (use this when committing to a direction)

```
You are a research strategist helping me find the intersection
between who I am and where the world needs me most. Treat this
as a deep research task: use real sources, cite where conversations
are happening, and surface concrete opportunities I can act on.

## About me

Skills (what I'm actually good at, technical and otherwise):
- [list 4-6]

Talents (what comes naturally — things I do well without trying hard):
- [list 3-5]

Interests (what I read, watch, or think about for fun):
- [list 4-6]

Fields I've already considered:
- [list any]

## What I want you to do

1. Map my profile to fields. Identify 6-10 distinct fields where
   my combination of skills + talents + interests is genuinely valuable.
   Don't include obvious matches only — surface 2-3 unexpected ones
   I probably haven't considered.

2. For each field, find real gaps. Where are people in that field
   RIGHT NOW saying they need solutions? Cite specific sources:
   subreddits, Twitter accounts, recent papers, news stories,
   conferences.

3. Connect my profile to the gaps. For each gap, name SPECIFICALLY
   why my skills + talents + interests let me approach it differently
   than someone without my profile.

4. Surface concrete next moves. For each field-gap pair, give me:
   - 2-3 specific people to follow or research
   - 1 specific community to join
   - 1 specific book, paper, or podcast to consume
   - 1 specific 30-day project to test the fit
   - 1 specific business idea to explore if the project lands

5. Rank the opportunities. Final ranked list with reasoning.

## Output format

### Field 1: [Name]
- Why this fits me
- The gap (with cited sources)
- My differentiation
- Where the conversation is happening
- Concrete next moves

[Repeat for fields 2 through 6-10]

### Ranked opportunities
[Ordered list with reasoning]

### What I haven't told you that might help
[3-5 questions to refine further]
```

## Quick-ideation variant (use for first pass / Day-30 follow-up)

```
I'm trying to figure out which field to go deeper in. Here's me:

Skills: [list 3-5]
Talents: [list 3]
Interests: [list 3-5]

Give me 8 distinct field-and-direction combos where this profile
would let me build something nobody else is building. For each,
tell me one real conversation happening right now about a gap I
could fill (with the source), and one 30-day project that would
test if this is for me.
```

## Why each part is designed the way it is

- **"You are a research strategist"** — role assignment anchors the AI in research mode, not chat.
- **Splitting skills / talents / interests** — students conflate these. Skills are trained, talents are innate, interests pull. The triangulation is where unique opportunity lives.
- **"Don't include obvious matches only"** — forces the model past the lazy answer.
- **"Cite specific sources"** — turns the AI from speculator to research strategist.
- **"30-day project + business idea"** — bridges research to action.
- **"What I haven't told you that might help"** — forces the model to admit its gaps and gives you the next iteration loop.

prompts.title: Body-Smart Teaching
prompts.summary: Teach a concept as a 30-second physical gesture with right-vs-wrong feels you can test against your body.
prompts.body:
A learning prompt for anyone who learns through movement, gesture, and embodied action. Physical analogies and gestures land; sitting still and reading slides off. Paste your About You file before this prompt for best results.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 1 · Lesson 3 (How You Think) · Personal Operating System

## The prompt

```
[Paste your About You file here.]

I'm body smart — I learn by moving and doing. Teach me [CONCEPT]
like this:

1. Describe the concept as a physical action — what would I be
   DOING with my body if I were inside this idea?
2. Give me a 30-second gesture or sequence of gestures I could
   actually perform that maps to the concept. Hands, posture,
   weight shifts — be specific.
3. Then describe what it feels like when the action is done well
   versus when it's wrong — what's the physical difference?
4. Connect it to one sport, dance, or physical skill I already
   know. Use the comparison to teach me where the energy goes.
5. Ask me to perform the gesture once, then describe what I felt.
   Use what I describe to find the part I haven't internalized yet.

If I can't feel the difference, give me a smaller action that
isolates just that part.
```

## Notes for use

- The `[CONCEPT]` placeholder is whatever you're trying to learn — "momentum and impulse," "a sine wave," "leverage," "a chemical bond."
- After running the prompt, *actually perform the gesture.* The instruction isn't a metaphor — proprioception is the encoding.
- If you want a deeper version (especially if you are past school age or running this at work), add a sixth step: *"Now tell me what happens at the edge of my body's range — where does this concept stop being something I can feel?"* Knowing where embodied intuition stops protects you from mis-applying the feel.

prompts.title: Logic-Smart Teaching
prompts.summary: Teach a concept by deriving it from a smaller principle you already accept, then break it at an edge case.
prompts.body:
A learning prompt for anyone who trusts mechanism over metaphor. Derivation, worked numbers, and edge-case failure modes are what land; story explanations slide off. Paste your About You file before this prompt for best results.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 1 · Lesson 3 (How You Think) · Personal Operating System

## The prompt

```
[Paste your About You file here.]

I'm logic smart — I trust mechanism, not metaphor. Teach me [CONCEPT]
like this:

1. State the headline as a precise claim — what is true, under what
   conditions.
2. Derive it from a smaller, more obvious principle I already accept.
   Show the steps. Don't skip the move I'd ask "why?" on.
3. Show me one worked example with small numbers I can verify by hand.
4. Then break it: name one edge case where this principle stops
   working, and explain why.
5. Ask me a quiz question that tests whether I actually understood
   the mechanism — not whether I remember the words.

If I get something wrong, don't just correct me. Trace where my
reasoning went off, then re-ask.
```

## Notes for use

- The `[CONCEPT]` placeholder is whatever you're trying to learn — "backpropagation," "Nash equilibrium," "the photoelectric effect."
- Step 4 (edge case that breaks it) is the move logic brains use to actually believe the rule. Don't skip it.
- If you want a deeper version (especially if you are past school age or running this at work), add a sixth step: *"Now connect this to one other concept I already know — what's the deeper principle they share?"* Spotting cross-domain isomorphisms is what turns problem-solver into theorist.

prompts.title: Music-Smart Teaching
prompts.summary: Teach a concept as a chant with verse-chorus structure and one deliberate pattern break.
prompts.body:
A learning prompt for anyone who retains through rhythm and repetition. A 5–8 word chant plus a verse-chorus structure lands; flat lists slide off. Paste your About You file before this prompt for best results.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 1 · Lesson 3 (How You Think) · Personal Operating System

## The prompt

```
[Paste your About You file here.]

I'm music smart — I retain things that have rhythm and repetition.
Teach me [CONCEPT] like this:

1. Give me the headline as a single short chant — 5 to 8 words I
   could repeat as a beat.
2. Then give me a structure I can hold like a song: verse / chorus
   / bridge. The chorus is what repeats every time. The verses
   carry the new information.
3. Build in a memorable repeat — a phrase, a rhyme, a 3-beat
   pattern that returns at each step.
4. Then break the pattern on purpose at one point so I notice
   what's different and why.
5. Ask me to recite or hum the structure back, in order, without
   looking. Tell me which beat I missed.

If I lose the rhythm, tell me which verse to re-loop.
```

## Notes for use

- The `[CONCEPT]` placeholder is whatever sequence you're trying to memorize — "the Krebs cycle," "the Bill of Rights," "Mandarin tones," a chord progression.
- Say the chant out loud at least three times after running the prompt. Silent reading defeats the format.
- If you want a deeper version (especially if you are past school age or running this at work), add a sixth step: *"Now tell me what genre this concept belongs to — what's the family of ideas it harmonizes with?"* Hearing the genre makes learning the next concept in it twice as fast.

prompts.title: Pattern-Smart Teaching
prompts.summary: Teach a concept by placing it in a family with siblings, a niche, and an ecosystem analogy.
prompts.body:
A learning prompt for anyone who thinks in classifications, ecosystems, and relationships (sometimes called *naturalistic*). Family trees and niche analogies land; isolated definitions slide off. Paste your About You file before this prompt for best results.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 1 · Lesson 3 (How You Think) · Personal Operating System

## The prompt

```
[Paste your About You file here.]

I'm pattern smart — I learn by placing things inside ecosystems
and classifications. Teach me [CONCEPT] like this:

1. State what this concept IS in one sentence, but also state its
   family — the larger category it belongs to.
2. Name 2–3 siblings — other concepts in the same family — and
   tell me what makes them different from this one.
3. Tell me what this concept is competing with, depending on, or
   eating. What's its niche?
4. Then describe an ecosystem or natural-world analogy where the
   same relationships play out — predator/prey, mutualism,
   succession, climate. Use a real system I'd recognize.
5. Ask me where else in life or in another field I'd expect this
   same pattern to show up. Score whether my answer fits.

If my analogy doesn't quite hold, tell me where the pattern breaks.
```

## Notes for use

- The `[CONCEPT]` placeholder is whatever you're trying to learn — "recurrent neural networks," "market makers," "monocots vs. dicots," "Romanticism."
- The "niche" question (step 3) is the encoding move. Knowing what something *competes with* or *depends on* tells you why it exists at all.
- If you want a deeper version (especially if you are past school age or running this at work), add a sixth step: *"Now name one cross-domain pattern this concept shares with a totally different field — and explain why the pattern is the same."* Cataloguing isomorphisms is how generalists get sharp.

prompts.title: People-Smart Teaching
prompts.summary: Teach a concept as a named argument between people who disagree, with stakes and a side to pick.
prompts.body:
A learning prompt for anyone who thinks through other people's perspectives (sometimes called *interpersonal*). Dialogue, conflict, and stakes land; abstract definitions slide off. Paste your About You file before this prompt for best results.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 1 · Lesson 3 (How You Think) · Personal Operating System

## The prompt

```
[Paste your About You file here.]

I'm people smart — I think through other people's perspectives.
Teach me [CONCEPT] like this:

1. Cast the concept as a conversation between two or three people
   who DISAGREE. Give each one a name, a role, and a stake.
2. Have them argue. Each person should make their strongest case
   in their own voice — quote them. Don't soften the disagreement.
3. Tell me what each person is worried about that the others
   aren't seeing.
4. Then tell me which person YOU think is right, and on what
   grounds. Don't hedge.
5. Ask me which character I'd want to be in this conversation and
   why. Then push back on my choice.

If I just pick the "winning" side, force me to defend the loser.
```

## Notes for use

- The `[CONCEPT]` placeholder is whatever you're trying to learn — "utilitarian vs. deontological ethics," "the gold standard debate," "the French Revolution," any policy or theoretical position.
- The push-back step (defend the loser) is the encoding move. Easy alignment is forgettable; defended alignment sticks.
- If you want a deeper version (especially if you are past school age or running this at work), add a sixth step: *"Now stage the same argument with one of the characters replaced by someone from history — who would they be, and how does the argument change?"* This is how you start seeing arguments as living traditions, not isolated ideas.

prompts.title: Picture-Smart Teaching
prompts.summary: Teach a concept as a sketchable diagram with an explicit order of drawing.
prompts.body:
A learning prompt for anyone who thinks in images and space. A diagram you can draw in 90 seconds lands; walls of text slide off. Paste your About You file before this prompt for best results.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 1 · Lesson 3 (How You Think) · Personal Operating System

## The prompt

```
[Paste your About You file here.]

I'm picture smart — I think in images and space. Don't explain
[CONCEPT] in paragraphs first. Teach me like this:

1. Describe a single diagram I can sketch in my notebook in about
   90 seconds. Tell me what boxes, arrows, and labels to use.
2. Give me the order of drawing — what goes down first, what gets
   added second, where each arrow points.
3. Tell me what stays in the picture and what disappears (what's
   the input, what's the output, what's the container).
4. Then describe what the diagram looks like when something goes
   wrong — what changes shape, breaks, or disappears.
5. Ask me three questions I should be able to answer from the
   picture alone, without re-reading any text.

If my answer doesn't match the picture, point to which box or
arrow I missed.
```

## Notes for use

- The `[CONCEPT]` placeholder is whatever you're trying to learn — "photosynthesis," "a transformer architecture," "supply and demand."
- After running the prompt, draw the diagram on actual paper before moving on. The drawing is the encoding.
- If you want a deeper version (especially if you are past school age or running this at work), add a sixth step: *"Now describe the same diagram at a different scale — zoom in on one box and tell me what's happening inside it."* Learning to switch scales is what turns "I drew it" into "I understand it."

prompts.title: Self-Smart Teaching
prompts.summary: Teach a concept against your own memory, decisions, and stated values.
prompts.body:
A learning prompt for anyone who thinks by going inward (sometimes called *intrapersonal*). Personal memory, value-alignment, and "what would I do differently" land; abstract definitions slide off. Paste your About You file before this prompt — this template specifically depends on it.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 1 · Lesson 3 (How You Think) · Personal Operating System

## The prompt

```
[Paste your About You file here.]

I'm self smart — I learn by connecting ideas to my own experience.
Teach me [CONCEPT] like this:

1. State the concept plainly in one sentence.
2. Then ask me to recall a moment from my own life where this
   concept was already at play, even if I didn't have a name for
   it. Don't move on until I answer.
3. Take what I said. Tell me what was actually happening
   underneath, in the language of the concept.
4. Then ask me: now that I have a name for it, what would I do
   differently the next time I encounter it?
5. Connect this to one value from my About You file. Tell me
   whether this concept supports that value, complicates it, or
   challenges it.

If my answer at step 2 is too abstract, push me for a specific
incident — a date, a place, a person.
```

## Notes for use

- The `[CONCEPT]` placeholder is whatever you're trying to learn — "sunk-cost fallacy," "Stoic acceptance," "scarcity mindset," a novel's central theme.
- This template depends harder on the About You file than any of the others — step 5 won't work without a stated value to anchor against.
- If you want a deeper version (especially if you are past school age or running this at work), add a sixth step: *"Now find a place in my About You file where this concept is operating RIGHT NOW — something I'm doing or deciding today where this idea applies."* That's where insight turns into a decision in the next 24 hours.

prompts.title: Word-Smart Teaching
prompts.summary: Teach a concept as story, metaphor, and re-explanation — the formats a language brain actually retains.
prompts.body:
A learning prompt for anyone who thinks in language. Story, metaphor, and conversation land; diagrams and equations slide off. Paste your About You file before this prompt for best results.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 1 · Lesson 3 (How You Think) · Personal Operating System

## The prompt

```
[Paste your About You file here.]

I'm word smart — I think in language. Don't show me diagrams or equations
first. Teach me [CONCEPT] like this:

1. Tell me the one-sentence headline I should remember.
2. Then explain it as a short story or scenario (3–5 sentences, no
   bullet points). Walk me through it the way you'd tell a friend.
3. Then give me the same concept told as a metaphor I'll remember —
   compare it to something I already understand from everyday life.
4. Then ask me to explain it back to you in my own words. Be honest
   about whether my explanation actually held up — if I left something
   out, tell me what's missing.

If I get stuck, don't just give me the answer. Ask me the question
that would unlock it.
```

## Notes for use

- The `[CONCEPT]` placeholder is whatever you're trying to learn — "photosynthesis," "Bayes' rule," "the second amendment."
- The fourth step (re-explanation) is the Feynman move. Don't skip it — that's where the concept actually consolidates.
- If you want a deeper version (especially if you are past school age or running this at work), add a fifth step: *"Now give me the same concept the way a critic would frame it — what's the one thing most people get wrong when they explain this?"* Critique-as-explanation sharpens word brains the fastest.

prompts.title: PHATE Deep Research
prompts.summary: Decode a field by its People, History, Arguments, Terms, and Events.
prompts.body:
A structured deep-research prompt for getting oriented in a brand-new field fast. PHATE = People, History, Arguments, Terms, Events — the five anchors you need before you can hold a real conversation in any field.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 3 · Lesson 1 (Field Decoding) · Research Block

## The prompt

```
You are a field guide writing a primer for a smart outsider
(me) who needs to be conversational in [FIELD] within two weeks.
Treat this as deep research — cite specific people, papers,
threads, podcasts, and events. No hand-waving.

Map the field along five anchors. For each one give me real
names and links, not summaries.

## 1. People

The 5-10 figures whose names you cannot avoid in this field.
For each:
- One-line position (academic, founder, critic, journalist)
- The one piece they're most known for (with link)
- One unexpected take they've held that defines them
- Where they post / publish today (Twitter, Substack, lab page)

## 2. History

A timeline of the 6-10 inflection points that made this field
what it is. For each:
- The year and event
- Why it mattered then
- Why it still matters now
- The primary source if there is one

## 3. Arguments

The 4-6 unresolved debates the field is actively having.
For each:
- The two camps, named, with their representatives
- The strongest case from each side, fairly
- Where the debate is currently happening (specific venue)
- Why an outsider might be useful here

## 4. Terms

The 15-20 terms I will hear in any serious conversation. For each:
- The definition (one sentence)
- The closest layperson translation
- The common misuse to watch for

## 5. Events

The 3-5 conferences, workshops, or recurring forums where
the field gathers. For each:
- Name, frequency, venue
- Who shows up
- Whether outsiders can attend
- The 1-2 talks from recent years worth watching

## Final section

What would you advise me to read FIRST, in order, over the
next 14 days to be conversational? Give me a 14-day reading
list with one anchor item per day.
```

## Notes for use

- The frontmatter `[FIELD]` should be replaced with the specific field — `complex systems`, `prediction markets`, `mechanism design`, etc.
- This prompt is the receipt students hand to themselves before any Stanford Profs outreach. You can't write a credible cold email until you can name 3-4 people in the field correctly.

prompts.title: Stanford Profs Outreach
prompts.summary: Draft a specific, non-generic cold email to a professor in your field.
prompts.body:
A cold-email drafting prompt for reaching out to professors (or any practitioner) whose work sits at the center of the field you're trying to enter. Optimized for *response*, not for *appearing impressive*.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 4 · Lesson 1 (Outreach) · Direct Practice

## The prompt

```
You are a cold-email coach who has gotten responses from 100+
busy academics. Help me draft an outreach email that gets a
real reply (not a polite no-reply).

Here is who I'm writing to:

Name: [Professor's full name]
Institution: [University]
Their work: [2-3 sentences about what they study]
Specific paper / talk / project of theirs I've engaged with: [be specific]
What surprised, confused, or hooked me about it: [be specific]

Here is who I am:

What I'm working on: [1-2 sentences]
Why their work matters for what I'm doing: [1-2 sentences]
What I'm specifically asking for: [must be one concrete ask]
What I'm NOT asking for: [also list — to show respect for their time]

## Draft the email with these constraints

- Subject line: 6 words max. Specific to their work, not generic.
- Opening: One specific sentence proving I read their work. Quote
  a line, name a result, reference a tweet — something that could
  not have been written by anyone else.
- Body: 3-4 short paragraphs. No flattery. No "huge fan." Tell them
  what I'm doing and why their work intersects.
- The ask: ONE concrete request. A 15-minute call. A pointer to
  one paper. An intro to one person. Never "any advice you have."
- Close: An exit ramp. "Totally understand if you're too busy" —
  in your own words.
- No signature beyond name and one link to my work.

## Critique pass

After drafting, audit your own email:
- Could this have been sent to any of their colleagues unchanged? If yes, rewrite.
- Is there a flattery word I could cut? (Cut it.)
- Is the ask concrete enough that they can say yes or no in one sentence?
- Is there a sentence the professor would skim past? (Cut it.)

Give me the final version after critique, plus a 2-line summary
of what you changed and why.
```

## Variant: practitioner DM (X / LinkedIn)

```
Same setup as above, but:
- Compress to 4 sentences total
- No subject line, no signature
- One concrete ask
- Lead with the specific thing they said that I'm responding to
- End with the ask
```

## What separates a reply from a no-reply

The single biggest predictor of a reply is the opening sentence proving you actually read their work. Most cold emails fail here. They open with "I'm a student interested in your research." That sentence could be sent to any of 500 professors. The professor knows it, and archives the email in 0.4 seconds.

Replace it with: *"Your 2023 paper on [exact title] made the claim that [specific result], which I'd been assuming was settled the other way after reading [other person's] work."* — and you get replies.

prompts.title: Synthesis Response Prompt
prompts.summary: Compress your About You file into a 100-word standing instruction you paste at the top of every future Claude chat.
prompts.body:
The condensation step. After your About You file exists, you don't want to paste a six-paragraph essay into every Claude conversation — you want the **shortest possible standing instruction** that still teaches the model how to talk to you. This meta-prompt interviews you briefly and produces that paragraph. Save the output as a separate file on your `Reflection` shelf — that's the file you copy-paste at the start of every real conversation going forward.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 2 · Lesson 2 (Compression) · Standing Instructions

## The prompt

```
You are helping me compress my full About You file into a 100-word
standing-instructions paragraph that I will paste at the top of
every future Claude or ChatGPT chat.

Operating rules:
1. Don't fabricate. If I don't tell you something, leave it out.
2. The output must be ONE paragraph, no bullets, no headers, no
   section breaks. It has to read like a single instruction a model
   can absorb in one breath.
3. Default to picture-smart phrasing unless I tell you otherwise.
4. Be ruthless about cuts. Anything that isn't load-bearing for
   "how should AI talk to me" gets cut.

Run this in three passes.

PASS 1 — Intake

Ask me to paste my full About You file. Wait for it. Once I paste
it, confirm what you see and ask me ONE question:
"Which two of the six fields are most load-bearing for HOW AI
should talk to you?" (Usually field 4 — intelligence — and field 6 —
how I want AI to operate — but not always.)

PASS 2 — Triage

Based on my answer, draft a first version. Constraints:
- Open with my name and one identity line (sport, school, or
  niche — pick the most distinctive).
- Name my intelligence profile in one phrase.
- State my standing instruction for how AI should operate (style,
  challenge, honesty).
- Name 1–2 things I'm currently working on that AI should know
  about.
- Cap at 100 words.

Show me the draft. Ask me what to cut and what's missing.

PASS 3 — Tighten

Take my edits. Produce a final version. Then output a single
markdown file I can save in my repo, using this EXACT schema:

=== FILE: prompts/My_Standing_Instructions.md ===
---
title: My Standing Instructions
shelf: Reflection
session: 2
lesson: 2
summary: The 100-word context I paste at the top of every Claude chat.
date_filed: [today, YYYY-MM-DD]
intelligence: [my primary intelligence]
---

The compressed version of my About You file. Paste this at the top
of every new conversation with Claude, ChatGPT, or any other model.
Refresh it whenever my life changes meaningfully.

**Sits in the curriculum at:** Session 2 · Lesson 2 (Compression)

## The prompt

[the final 100-word paragraph, in a code fence so I can copy it
cleanly]


## How to use

- Paste the contents of the code fence at the top of every real
  Claude conversation, before I ask my first question.
- Update this file when my life changes — new project, new
  intelligence sub-skill, new stake.
- Compare versions over time. The diff between today's and the
  one I write in six months is a portrait of how I grew.

After the file block, give me one line: "Save this file at the
path shown, commit it, and your standing instructions card
appears on your Reflection shelf within a minute."

Start now by asking me to paste my About You file.
```

## Notes for use

- Run this only after `Personal_Context_Bootstrap.md` has produced your full `About_You.md`. The compression doesn't work without the source material.
- The 100-word cap is real. If the model produces 140, push back. Compression is the point.
- Re-run this every 60–90 days. The version you produce today will read embarrassingly off-base by the time you ship your first portfolio project — that's the signal it worked.
- For 17–20-year-olds: after Pass 3, ask the model for an *alternate* version that strips your sport / school / niche and writes a "professional-context-only" variant. You'll want both — one for personal exploration, one for things headed to a recommender's inbox.
